content:
Repository Pattern

Software Architecture

Programming

Aspnetcore

4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

A few years ago, I thought the Repository Pattern was the answer to every database problem.
Starting a new project? Add a repository.
Building an API? Add a repository.
Creating a small CRUD application? You guessed it — add a repository.
It felt like the “professional” thing to do because almost every tutorial I watched had an IRepository<T> somewhere in the project.
Then I started working on larger enterprise applications.
That’s when I realized something surprising:
The Repository Pattern isn’t always the right solution. Sometimes it makes your application cleaner. Other times, it only adds unnecessary complexity.
In this article, I’ll explain what the Repository Pattern really is, why developers use it, where it shines, and the situations where I’d avoid it.

First, What Is a Repository?
Think about ordering food online.
You don’t call the restaurant’s kitchen directly.
Instead, you place your order through the app. The app talks to the restaurant, the kitchen prepares the food, and eventually the food reaches you.
You don’t need to know how the kitchen works.
The app acts as the middleman.
A repository plays a very similar role.
Instead of your business logic talking directly to the database, it talks to a repository.
Instead of writing SQL queries or Entity Framework code everywhere, your application asks the repository for data.
For example:
var customer = await _customerRepository.GetByIdAsync(id);The service doesn’t care whether the data comes from SQL Server, PostgreSQL, an API, or even a file.
It simply asks for a customer.

Without a Repository
Imagine you have a service like this:
public class CustomerService
{
private readonly AppDbContext _context; public CustomerService(AppDbContext context)
{
_context = context;
} public async Task<Customer> GetCustomer(int id)
{
return await _context.Customers
.FirstOrDefaultAsync(x => x.Id == id);
}
}This works perfectly.
There is nothing wrong with this code.
In fact, for many applications, this is all you need.
So why introduce another layer?

With a Repository
Now let’s move the data access into a repository.
public interface ICustomerRepository
{
Task<Customer?> GetByIdAsync(int id);
}Implementation:
public class CustomerRepository : ICustomerRepository
{
private readonly AppDbContext _context; public CustomerRepository(AppDbContext context)
{
_context = context;
} public async Task<Customer?> GetByIdAsync(int id)
{
return await _context.Customers
.FirstOrDefaultAsync(x => x.Id == id);
}
}Now the service becomes much cleaner.
public class CustomerService
{
private readonly ICustomerRepository _repository; public CustomerService(ICustomerRepository repository)
{
_repository = repository;
} public async Task<Customer?> GetCustomer(int id)
{
return await _repository.GetByIdAsync(id);
}
}The service no longer knows anything about Entity Framework.
Its only responsibility is business logic.

Why Developers Like Repositories
One reason is organization.
Imagine a project that’s been running for five years.
Instead of database queries being scattered across dozens of services, all the data access lives in one place.
Need to optimize a query?
There’s usually only one file to update.
Need to add logging?
You know exactly where to do it.
Need to change how data is retrieved?
Again, one place.
That alone can make large projects easier to maintain.

It Also Makes Testing Easier
Suppose your service calculates discounts for premium customers.
You don’t really want your unit test connecting to a real database every time it runs.
Instead, you can replace the repository with a fake implementation.
_repository.GetByIdAsync(1)can simply return a sample customer.
Your test focuses only on the business logic.
No SQL Server.
No Entity Framework.
No database setup.
Tests become faster and much easier to write.

Here’s Where Things Go Wrong
This is where many projects make a mistake.
Developers create something like this:
public interface IRepository<T>
{
GetAll();
GetById();
Add();
Update();
Delete();
}At first, it looks elegant.
One repository for everything.
But after a few months, reality kicks in.
Customers need custom searches.
Orders need filtering.
Products need sorting.
Reports require complex joins.
Now the generic repository starts growing dozens of special methods.
Soon you have methods like:
GetCustomersWithPendingInvoices()GetOrdersCreatedBetween()GetProductsWithSupplier()GetCustomersByRegion()The “generic” repository isn’t generic anymore.
It has become a dumping ground for unrelated queries.

Isn’t Entity Framework Already a Repository?
This question comes up a lot.
And honestly…
It’s a fair question.
DbSet<T> already behaves like a repository.
It lets you:

• Query data

• Add records

• Update records

• Delete records
Many experienced .NET developers therefore skip writing another repository layer altogether.
Instead, they inject DbContext directly into their services.
For small and medium-sized applications, that’s often a perfectly reasonable approach.
Adding another abstraction just because “everyone does it” doesn’t necessarily improve the design.

So When Should You Use the Repository Pattern?
I’d seriously consider it if:

• The project is expected to live for many years.

• Multiple developers are working on it.

• The application has complex business rules.

• Data comes from different sources.

• Database access needs to be isolated from business logic.
These are situations where the extra layer pays for itself.

When I’d Skip It
On the other hand, I probably wouldn’t create repositories for:

• Small CRUD projects

• Internal admin panels

• Simple Web APIs

• Personal side projects

• Prototypes

• Applications that are almost entirely Entity Framework queries
Sometimes keeping things simple is the better engineering decision.

The Biggest Lesson I Learned
Early in my career, I believed good architecture meant adding more layers.
Today, I believe good architecture means adding only the layers that solve real problems.
The Repository Pattern is a tool.
Like every tool, it’s useful when used in the right place.
A hammer is fantastic for driving nails.
It isn’t very useful for tightening a screw.
The same idea applies here.
If your application genuinely benefits from separating data access, use a repository.
If Entity Framework already provides everything you need, don’t be afraid to keep your design simple.
Good software isn’t measured by how many patterns it contains.
It’s measured by how easy it is for the next developer — including your future self — to understand, maintain, and improve.
